topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on my screen, a stark, unwelcome intrusion into the otherwise quiet evening. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a friend request. The name staring back at me was a familiar, unwelcome ghost from elementary school—Mark. My breath hitched, catching in my throat. I stared at the profile picture, a slightly distorted, overly confident grin I remembered vividly from years of playground dominance. My fingers hovered over the 'decline' button, trembling slightly. Why now? After all this time? A wave of prickling awareness washed over me, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I quickly minimized the message, shoving my phone face down on the desk, trying to force my breathing to slow down. The silence of my room suddenly felt too loud, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, and the sight of the request made my hand freeze mid-air. A strange tension coiled in my shoulders, pulling my neck taut. It was Mark. The memory of his sharp tongue and the way he used to corner me in the hallway surfaced instantly, bringing a flush to my cheeks. I reread the request twice, my eyes darting back and forth between the screen and the closed door of my bedroom. A tremor ran through my hands, making the phone feel slippery. I didn't know what to do with it. Should I ignore it completely, hoping it would vanish like a bad dream? Or should I open it? The hesitation was palpable; every muscle in my body seemed locked, waiting for a signal that wouldn't come. I finally let the phone drop onto the duvet, the sound muffled, and just lay there, staring at the ceiling, feeling a distinct pressure building behind my eyes.

[story 3]

When the alert sounded, I instinctively pulled my phone away from my lap as if it were suddenly hot. My heart began to beat a rapid, uneven rhythm against my ribs. The name was there: Mark. The familiar sting of old antagonism flared up, making my jaw tighten. I felt a sudden urge to check the lock on my door, a nervous habit I hadn't indulged in for years. Every nerve ending seemed to be firing at once. I could feel a distinct lightness in my chest, like I was holding my breath underwater. I didn't want to interact with this. I slowly reached for the notification, my movements stiff and deliberate, as if navigating a minefield. The screen glowed, and for a moment, the world narrowed down to that single, unwelcome invitation.
